item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Per consensus of Licensing staff, this image is assumed to be self-owned by the author and thus is compliant with CC 3.0.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok. The idea was definitely interesting, but there are some ways that just made it feel, off. But it was good overall

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to believe this is a Daevite artifact.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 'nearly exactly 115.57cm'? May I remove the 'nearly'?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know, even though I am a big advocate of scary SCPs, a temple made out of either human skulls or pickles is pretty scary, like all the stuff in the last experiment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the progression and the last log, also the expungements are well-placed in my opinion, they really spark some more interest as they should. The last note is hard to read, though and doesn't hint a lot. What I however applaud is the creativity with the recovery document. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
… nearly exactly 115.57 cm from end to end…

It's either nearly or exactly, but probably not both. As the measure is given to two decimal places, I'm leaning toward "exactly."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If the subject lacks a formal secondary or higher education, they will instead feel compelled to speak on a subject that they personally believe themselves to be knowledgeable on.

Insert joke about this being me on a normal day.

But seriously, I love random weirdness. I love fish raining from the sky for fuck-if-we-know. And this one is still just too disjunct for me. Why the pit stop at Methodism? Why the meandering, disjointed effects? Maybe if they were more compelling individually, this would work— but it just doesn't gel into real intrigue or eeriness, for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, but there are some writing/tone hiccoughs that bother me.

After four hours, twenty three minutes the religious acts described by the subject no longer resemble any known existing or former religions, including elaborate human sacrifices taking place in temples carved from [DATA EXPUNGED]

That is not good expungement.

Security team is instructed to enter the testing chamber and open fire on subject. [DATA EXPUNGED] containment procedures engaged [DATA EXPUNGED] 37 minutes later, using coordinated use of high explosives. Other than a layer of explosives residue, SCP-1430 remains undamaged. Subject D-13742 terminated.

See above comment.

Really the whole final paragraph strikes me as not good tone.

Why are we testing this on agents first?

I don't think the last scanned document is necessary either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Was the mace a gift of a person or group?"

"WHO CARES"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the last few words are, "make yourself a copy". But I can't make out the rest of the handwriting. The scanned document, overall, is odd, and I don't know if it's intended to seem odd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) I can't read the handwriting on the scanned document. What's that say?
2) What was the inspiration item/institution? I feel like I should recognize it from the emblem, but it's not coming to me.

Good job with the transistion from odd to dangerous. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "tagme" maybe?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey Bob, we got this SCP that just came in. Seems to make people crazy or something.

Really Mitch? Fuck, let's let the boys in the lab hold it to see what happens.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It seems to me that too much of the containment procedures should actually be part of the description. Here's my take:

Special Containment Procedures: SCP-1430 is to be stored in a security code locked container.

Fine, but:

Direct contact with SCP-1430 for longer than four hours can cause irreversible psychological damage.

That's a description of what the item does, not what we need to do to contain it. I'd reword this to something like "No personnel are to have physical contact with SCP-1430 for longer than four hours cumulative other than for testing purposes."

Additionally, SCP-1430’s effects will override an affected subject’s ability to self-censor, and may result in unintentional divulgence of highly classified information. For this reason, personnel above a level 1 security clearance are to avoid direct contact with the object.

Again, the first half is a description, not the containment procedure.

As of ██/██/2009, only D-class subjects are to be used for experimentation.
As of ██/██/2010, per directive by site command, experimentation with SCP-1430 is to be discontinued indefinitely.

The rest of the containment procedures should be good.

Now, I know you fleshed it out based upon other feedback about it being "criminally short", but the way I see it is that it shouldn't be just a cut and paste job from the description. The containment procedures should give just enough information to successfully contain the SCP without actually revealing anything extra about the object in question; this should especially go for a SCP which personnel with any clearance aren't allowed to handle, as for actual containment, the only personnel handling the SCP might not even have access to read the rest of the document.

From a meta-standpoint, the containment procedures should in my view primarily be used to pique the interest of the reader. When they start the description section, they should be wondering why personnel can't handle it for longer than 4 hours, and why you can't let anyone with a clearance touch it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really liked this until the awkward expungement toward the end. Now I just kind of like it, but it's enough for an upvote.

After four hours, twenty three minutes the religious acts described by the subject no longer resemble any known existing or former religions, including elaborate human sacrifices taking place in temples carved from Hershey's milk chocolate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A Spruce Goose with the personality of Gen. Patton… hmmm…

<distant sounds of scribbling>

:)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I very much like that it isn't "talk until you die" instantly. The last log added a lot to this whole thing; I have to admit, I found the description itself pretty bland until I saw the thing in action.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aight. On my second readthrough of this, I gotta say, I like it. Hyaenaboy isn't wrong, but I feel… nostalgic is the right word? I remember liking SCPs like this, and it feels nice to read it. Good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe we should have a tag that calls for Mack or equivalent to come and review an SCP's tags if a user is unsure about them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I believe it is poor form to just tag pages as scp and the Object Class. If it's untagged, Mack and whoever else can find it using Nala, but articles with non-extensive tags slip in under the radar. I believe so, at least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Grrr… The advice to "don't tag your own" is only half-true. Don't tag your own if you're not sure which ones are applicable. Read the Tag Guide if you want to see acceptable ones, and don't make up your own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Remember when SCPs used to be frightening?1 This just bores me. In short, it's a random object that compulses you to do something until you die, but not before it talks about scawee ancient practices of murder and sacrifice. And another language that "we do not yet understand dun dun". I mean, the idea behind the object is interesting itself, but so is a giant sentient winged aircraft made out of wood with the personality of General Patton.2

In short, it's written very well, but I groaned at the idea and the payoff.

Footnotes
1. In before "Every SCP doesn't have to be scary."
2. Scratch that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Don't get me wrong, I have nothing against religion. My parents are Episcopalians. But can't this guy just live and let live? This Jesus stuff ain't for everyone, know what I'm sayin? Oh, and that human sacrifice shit is pretty weird too.

Edit: -__-

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel it could still use some polishing in the writing and redactions, but it's not bad over all. The expungement at the end of the log actually doesn't actively bother me, but I do feel something more interesting could have gone there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 1722 vs 1430! Buy your tickets now!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One stick, two stick, now all we need is a red stick and a blue stick…

But yeah, overall, pretty good article, interesting effect, it kept my attention, but I feel the expungements near the end kinda kill the excitement, though I will admit that I wanna know why the hell they had to use explosives.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This got really weird near the end. I do agree with the others about the expungement, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually kind of like this. My main issue is the completely unnecessary use of redactions through the article; none of your expungements particularly need to be there, and just break up the narrative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hm. On one hand, the execution needs work and the ending was predictable, but on the other hand, it's interesting. Tentative upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mostly like it, but the ending seemed…meh. I like the whole thing enough to upvote, but it's not the best, really.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh man, it's way too late for me to be doing this but:
I fixed up a lot of the things that seemed to be big glaring issues with sentence structure and cut the whole thing down a bunch, and (hopefully) fixed up all the formatting problems.
Thanks for all the input, folks. I 'preciate it a bunch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like doctor notes on the test logs have fallen out of favor, flavor-wise.

Everyone else with much more experience and writing skill have given good style tips.
Trimming and editing out fluff statements is a good habit because it makes the embellishments that you choose to retain stand out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sweet! Thanks. Like I said in response to Scantron, I've already pulled a lot of the junk info and the nonscientific phrases for a second draft.
The info about the containment procedure is super helpful. I'll be sure to revise it.

As for "a ceremonial mace resembling a staff", I guess I included that because technically a ceremonial mace and a staff are different things, but in retrospect yeah, it does look pretty ugly. I'll drop it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It being 9 a.m. in the morning for me, I'll see what I can do about a line-by-line critique.

+ Critique
- Hide 

Special containment procedures: SCP-1430 is to be stored in a security code locked, upright, opaque container that allows no rotational movement of any kind. Presently held in Sector 28.

Formatting is off here (no capitalisation on the heading) and this is criminally short. There are loads of other things you need to mention, based on the article itself. I'll point them out as I go through.

a ceremonial mace resembling a staff

I have know idea why this needs to be written like this. Just say a ceremonial mace or a staff.

115.57 cm (45.5 in)

Don't use imperial measurements in articles, even if you're sneakily hiding them in parentheses. As somebody else said, the Foundation writes in Standard Index figures, and they shouldn't need to add an aside saying how much the object weighs in apples.

and weighing anywhere between 1.3 and 3.2 kg (the weight of SCP-1430 is believed to fluctuate as a byproduct of its unique properties. The weights listed are the highest and lowest recorded, but mathematical models suggest they may have a range from 340 grams to 6.1 kg)

This could be structured better to removed the awkward parenthesis.

as well as a woman holding a [REDACTED]

This is a very silly piece of expungement. When I saw this, this is how I imagine the archival for this item going:

Transcript of initial item appraisal for SCP-1430, performed by Dr. J. Williams and Research Assistant K. Epps.

<Begin Transcript>

(1 Minute and 19 seconds redacted for brevity)

"Doctor Williams, what's that she's holding? The woman in the carving?"

"Well Kenneth, it looks like… oh God."

"Sir?"

"Good God, Kenneth, I can't believe it! She's… she's holding a [REDACTED]!"

"Jesus Christ! I think I'm going to be… (sound of vomiting Research Assistant Epps vomiting)"

"I.. I… No-one can ever know the truth. It's too much, they couldn't handle it. No one else can ever know. It's too dangerous."

"What are you saying, Doctor?"

"Expunge everything related to it, remove it from the record. We can't even risk other personnel finding it out. It's too much."

"Ugh…"

"I need to go home and take a shower. Testing adjourned. Fucking hell."

<End Transcript>

Hopefully that should demonstrate why this expungement is really silly.

from virtually unnoticeable unless in little to no light, to roughly the intensity “of a dim flashlight”.

That is not a scientific way to describe the phenomenon. I'll go back to the 'we don't measure mass in apples' metaphor as an explanation of why this isn't a good description.

At this point, it is still safe to remove SCP-1430 from the subject without permanent psychological damage

I'd personally assume, given that it was not mentioned anywhere previously, that it is still okay to remove the item. Putting this in is redundant to what comes immediately after this, and reads oddly.

At this point, separating SCP-1430 from the subject will cause permanent psychological and neurological damage.

You should probably address this in the containment procedures, i.e. "SCP-1430 should not be handled by personnel for more than 3 hours at a time."

Notes: 1430 seems to override self-censorship. I suggest we don’t allow it to come in contact with anyone privy to knowledge that might be considered sensitive. -Dr. ████

That sounds like something that should be mentioned in the containment procedures.

That’s it, from now on we’re only allowing D-classes for experiments

Again, should be in the containment procedures.

“cons”

Pointless scare quotes. If at any point you feel the need to put something into scare quotes, it's my opinion that you should ask yourself whether you are recording the testimony of a person, or just trying to make up for it sounding non-clinical. If you are trying to make for it sounding non-clinical, see if you can think of a way to say it clinically (like 'scam' for example, seeing as you used that one a lot).

Notes: Following the incidents of test 1430-17, testing on SCP-1430 has been halted by site command.

Again, should be mentioned in the containment procedures.

Despite that, I'd say you have got much better writing style than I would expect from someone committing these sorts of errors, and that you've got a good idea here. This article is largely good, but there are certainly some things that need to be fixed up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok wow, this is awesome.

The second paragraph of the article, and the details with the light, etc. was actually what I originally wrote as the starting idea. I was this close to cutting it before I submitted, and I think now I've got no problems with dropping it for good.

Informal quotations were something I was a little unsure about as I was writing, but if the group consensus is that they're inappropriate, I can easily change those out.

I think that formatting, if I were to be so bold as to submit self-criticism, is my biggest problem. I anticipate spending much time reading and re-reading the formatting page in the near future.

And… I don't really remember what those boxes were blanking out. I'm sure it was something obscene, though.

Anyway, thanks a bunch for the input. A second draft of this, I think, will solve a whole bunch stuff that's kind of frustratingly obvious in hindsight.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know, I kind of like the idea. What really kills this for me is the writing1:

  • Lots of informal quotations in place of a clinical explanation of what it does.
    • One subject described rotating SCP-1430 as “like swinging a baseball bat underwater”.
    • to roughly the intensity “of a dim flashlight”
    • though with a stilted, “unpracticed” or “unnatural” voice
    • but instead attempts to “speak”
    • Subject occasionally coughs mid “word” to spit up blood.
  • Some difficulties with sentence structure.2
    • Adorning the top is the seal of the University of ████████, the original location of the object's retrieval, the figure of a adult male breaking away from chains, presumably the “bonds of ignorance” described in an associated recovered document, as well as a woman holding a [REDACTED] emits faint visible light when in low light conditions, or exposed to certain chelating agents.
    • However, after three hours and forty seven minutes, subject’s narrative takes an unexpected turn, no longer instructing listeners to seek guidance, but instead insisting on self-mutilation, or other violent punishments for increasingly mundane “sins”, differing greatly from standard Methodist doctrine.
  • Unnecessary sentences that clutter up the piece from a literary standpoint. Less is more.
    • The entire second paragraph.
    • The light at the tip of it. Blatant literary device, no obvious point from an in-universe standpoint.
    • Chosen topics of speech have included abstract mathematics, molecular biology, “jerry-rigging”, military tactics, the construction and use of shanks, religious studies, and “gettin’ them █████ ███ ████”. This effect extends to subjects who would normally not choose to speak publicly, including self described introverts, and after sufficient exposure even psychologically traumatized subjects who, under normal circumstances would be effectively mute, will speak (though with a stilted, “unpracticed” or “unnatural” voice).3
  • Bad formatting that, to an experienced reader, is grating. We have a template on the How to Write an SCP page, and plenty of articles to look to for examples.
    • Object #:
    • Special containment procedures:
    • Object Description:
    • No spacing in the collapsed logs.
  • Misuse of 'however'. Incorrect: "X, however Y". Correct: "X; however, Y". That always grinds my goat.

And, uh, are those boxes blacking out "bitch ass hoes"? :I

Anyways, -1. I'm not entirely sure what to think, because although you did a lot (really, a lot) of things wrong that would normally point to sloppy writing and low effort, your attention to detail at some points was refreshing, and clearly you have lots of imagination and put lots of work into this. Keep practicing, I'm sure we'll get something good out of you some day.

Footnotes
1. This is not a comprehensive list.
2. Each sentence gets one idea, and one paragraph gets a related set of ideas. Each sentence has to flow logically to the next, and the paragraphs should be arranged to form the most logical structure for the piece.
3. You could simply cut these lines and change "held by a subject" to "held by any subject" and remove whole sentences that add nothing to your piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pulled the tags. Seems to me like tag etiquette isn't clearly laid out in the Guide to Newbies? Whatever, won't be making that mistake again.

Thanks for adding the rating module; I knew I was forgetting something. Hopefully also won't make that mistake again either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Boyo, you didn't include the rating module, your formatting is shit, your containment procedures are odd and it's three A.M. in the fuckin' morning. -1 for right now. I might look over it again tomorrow and do a line-by-line. Goodnight.

Also, TAGS. ARE. INCORRECT.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kinda like it, but the language is a little bit rough in ways I can't quite put a finger on. I'm sure more experienced people will help you feex it :)
Also, ratin' module, where art thou [added rating module ]

P.S.:
Don't tag your own skips. McKenzie here is the tag deity, leave it to her
